coredata.dc:description: The excessive industrial applications of methylene blue dye and lead ions with their high resistance to biodegradation in water systems have led to their detection in water effluents as pollutants of major concern and therefore they must be removed by efficient techniques. Carbonaceous based materials as carbon foam (C-Foam) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with sodium alginate are important reactive adsorbents in water treatment. Therefore, a novel nanocomposite was assembled via crosslinking of zinc oxide with carbon foam, GQDs and alginate to fabricate ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate. This nanocomposite exhibited homogeneous and spherical particles with 20 nm average size. ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate is designed to remove Pb(II) and methylene blue (MB) pollutants. The collected results referred to the optimum pH 6 and 7 for Pb(II) and MB, respectively. The computed thermodynamic parameters revealed endothermic and spontaneous reactions by the two pollutants providing ΔGo from −8.06 to −13.67 kJ/mol for lead ion and from −6.23 to −8.19 kJ/mol for MB dye. The interfering species exhibited almost insignificant impact. ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite confirmed successful reusability for five successive regeneration cycles. Finally, ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite provided excellent removal trends for Pb(II) and MB pollutants from real water samples with100% and 92.2 % removal, respectively.

head.ce:title: Engineering nanocomposite of graphene quantum dots/carbon foam/alginate/zinc oxide beads for efficacious removal of lead and methylene

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The excessive industrial applications of methylene blue dye and lead ions with their high resistance to biodegradation in water systems have led to their detection in water effluents as pollutants of major concern and therefore they must be removed by efficient techniques. Carbonaceous based materials as carbon foam (C-Foam) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with sodium alginate are important reactive adsorbents in water treatment. Therefore, a novel nanocomposite was assembled via crosslinking of zinc oxide with carbon foam, GQDs and alginate to fabricate ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate. This nanocomposite exhibited homogeneous and spherical particles with 20 nm average size. ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate is designed to remove Pb(II) and methylene blue (MB) pollutants. The collected results referred to the optimum pH 6 and 7 for Pb(II) and MB, respectively. The computed thermodynamic parameters revealed endothermic and spontaneous reactions by the two pollutants providing ΔGo from −8.06 to −13.67 kJ/mol for lead ion and from −6.23 to −8.19 kJ/mol for MB dye. The interfering species exhibited almost insignificant impact. ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite confirmed successful reusability for five successive regeneration cycles. Finally, ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite provided excellent removal trends for Pb(II) and MB pollutants from real water samples with100% and 92.2 % removal, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Freshwater scarcity has become one of the most concern issues worldwide due to population growth, climate change as well as industrial and agricultural activities [1,2]. In addition, widespread pollution of water resources affected the aquatic environment via direct dumbing of wastes into water bodies without prior treatment processes [3–9]. In addition, the expansion in industrial activities generated serious and wide spread of heavy metal ions as well as organic pollutants [10–16]. Heavy metal ions in water and wastewater are generally resulted from various industrial activities including batteries fabrication and reuse, electroplating and many other sources [17]. Relying on their high solubility and mobility, heavy metal ions were identified by harmful effects on water bodies and the environment with serious effects on human health [18–20]. Lead is known as one of the most dangerous non-biodegradable heavy metals and widely employed in many industries including batteries fabrication and painting. However, bioaccumulations of lead in the aquatic systems were reported to enforce hazardous effects and health diseases as blood pressure, kidney, liver and nervous system damages [21]. Therefore, elimination of lead ions from water matrices is a target with major concern [22,23].

Nowadays thousands of dyes are manufactured for implementation in different industrial activities as papers, plastics, leather, pharmaceuticals, textiles, cosmetics and dyestuffs [24]. So, considerable amounts of colored wastewater are produced to cause direct contamination of water with nonbiodegradable, highly undesirable and toxic materials [25]. However, dyes are basically divided into anionic derivatives (direct, acidic and reactive dyes), cationic compounds (basic dyes) and nonionic species (dispersed dyes) [26]. Methylene blue dye (MB) is an example of cationic dyes with a great threat as a carcinogenic pollutant and strong harming effects on respiratory system as well as the environment [27]. MB is poorly biodegradable due to its aromaticity and could last for long time unless proper action is taken to remove it from aqueous matrices [24–27].

Carbonaceous based materials as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon foam (C-Foam) have attracted much attention and therefore, found recent interests due to their excellent properties and incorporated characteristics as variable oxygen functionalities, beside low toxicity and high mechanical and thermal stabilities [28–31]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown a wide range of applications due to their cost effective, pyrolytic synthesis and production by efficient thermal decomposition of a number of organic compounds (viz. citric acid, sugar, agriculture wastes and many other sources). The advantages of GQDs as adsorbent are related to surface oxygen functionalities and excellent efficacy in adsorption and removal of cationic species from aquatic matrices [18,28]. Therefore, fabricated GQDs from agriculture waste were previously used to remove lead and lanthanum ions with high adsorption capacity as 3400 μmol g−1 and 1500 μmol g−1, respectively. In addition, CQDs@PAFP nanocomposite was synthesized for removal of U(VI) [32], while GQDs-cellulose aerogel composite was used for removal and detection of trivalent chromium ion in water samples [33]. On the other hand, carbon foam (C-Foam) is another example of cost-effective alternative material which is characterized by ease and low-cost fabrication from numerous sources like sawdust, rice husk and sugar [2]. C-Foam is prepared by blowing and carbonization of carbon source material. It has spongy structure with interconnected macropores, very light weight, high porosity, thermally stability and large surface area [2]. Owing to such remarkable properties, C-Foam materials have found their ways in a variety of applications as catalysis, filtration, electrochemical reactions and adsorption process for removal of organic/inorganic pollutants from wastewater [24]. C-Foam/metal oxide composite was reported for removal of lead (II), copper (II) and chromium (III) ions with maximum of 170.85 mg g−1 for divalent lead ion [34]. Another prepared C-Foam showed higher capacity for removal of chromium and copper ions than cation exchange resin and therefore, it was reported for possible usage in wastewater treatment as nontoxic alternative [35].

Alginate in the form of sodium or potassium is a natural polysaccharide extracted from brown algae. Alginate salts are sustainable and renewable materials which are characterized with some outstanding properties including bio-degradability and bio-compatibility. Moreover, plenty of incorporated carboxyl and hydroxyl groups were found to force high adsorption affinity for heavy metals and organic pollutants [36]. However, the related properties to mechanical stability, strength and heat resistance are relatively low [37] and therefore, physical or chemical modification is generally executed to enhance its applicability in adsorptive removal of various pollutants. Generally, the employed modification methods of sodium alginate to fabricate alginate-based adsorbents were reported by using surface grafting as well as cross-linking, and composite formation with other materials as metal oxides [38,39].

To the best of our knowledge, the combination of GQDs with C-Foam, ZnO and alginate in a single nanocomposite was not previously reported. Therefore, a novel nanocomposite is aimed to design, assemble and characterize, in this study, based on the crosslinking of alginate with zinc oxide, carbon foam and graphene quantum dots for the fabrication of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate beads by using microwave green synthesis approach. ZnO/C-Foam was firstly prepared by blowing and carbonization of sucrose incorporated zinc nitrate and functionalized with GQDs for the formation of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs. Secondly, sodium alginate was crosslinked with ZnO/C-foam/GQDs for the production of the aimed nanocomposite beads. In addition, the assembled nanocomposite was not previously synthesized and applied in water treatment and therefore, it was aimed to employ as a multipurpose adsorbent material in adsorptive removal of cationic inorganic (Pb(II)) and organic (MB dye) pollutants from water. The controlling parameters were also optimized to evaluate the adsorption isotherm, thermodynamics, kinetics parameters and acting mechanisms (see Scheme 1 ).
Chemicals, materials, solutions and all applied instrumentations are listed in Table 1S with their specifications.

ZnO/C-Foam was prepared by the heat fusion of 0.3 g of sucrose with1.5 g of ZnNO3.6H2O on oil bath at 110 °C, till formation of a brown molten phase. This material was subjected to irradiation in a microwave oven at 110 °C for 10 min. Finally the produced black precipitate of ZnO/C-Foam was then cooled and collected [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from popcorn
ce:section.ce:para: 5.0 g of corn seeds were placed and heated for 5 min in a microwave oven to obtain corn kernel, and the powder was collected and grinded. This material (4.0 g) was then heated in a muffle furnace at 800 °C for 4 h to have finally carbon black. 3.0 g of carbon black was then mixed with 300 mL H2O2 (50 %) plus 2 drops of acetic acid and heated for 24 h at 50 °C. A yellow solution was then obtained and neutralized with 0.01 M NaOH solution to form GQDs and kept at 4 °C for further use [41].

0.3 g of ZnO/C-Foam was added to 50 mL of the prepared GQDs, sonicated for 5 min and subjected to good mixing and allowed to react for 90 min under magnetic stirring. Sodium alginate (1.0 g/100 mL aqueous solution) was then drop wisely added with continuous stirring for 120 min followed by syringe dropwise addition of this mixture to 150 mL of 1.0 M CaCl2 to result in the formation of brown black beads and left for 24 h. This mixture was filtered, washed several times with distilled water and dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain the target ZnO/C-foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite. The same procedure was also applied for preparation of alginate beads in absence of ZnO/C-Foam and GQDs.

Removal of both pollutants by the novel ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite was examined and optimized under various parameters including impact of pH, contact time, mass dosage, pollutants concentrations, temperature, and interfering ions. Lead ion was determined by atomic absorption spectroscopy at wavelength = 283.3 nm and methylene blue was determined by UV–Visible spectroscopy at λmax = 668 nm [42].

Impact of pH was accomplished by addition and shaking of 10 mg of ZnO/C-foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite with 10 mL of 10 mg L-1 pollutant (Pb(II) or MB dye) for 30 min. The pH 1–7 range was used for Pb(II) and the range of pH 1–9 was applied for MB. After adsorption, the nanocomposite was filtered and the removal (%R) was calculated from Eq. 1 (Table 1S, Supplementary material) [25]. To determine the point of zero charge (pHpzc) of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite, 0.1 mol/L HCl or 0.1 mol/L NaOH was mixed with 2.0 g nanocomposite and 50 mL of 0.01 mol/L of NaCl in order to have an initial pH 2–10 range, followed by shaking for 4 h, and after 24 h, the pHfinal was measured. Plotting the values of pHinitial against pH difference (pHfinal - pHinitial) was graphed to determine the pHpzc of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite [32].

Impact of contact time was done by changing the reaction time from 1-30 min for lead ion and MB dye using pH 6 and 7, respectively. 10 mL of 10 mg L-1 of both pollutants were shaken with 10 mg of the nanocomposite to identify the %R as described above. Mass dosage effect was done by using ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite masses ranging from 1 to 10 mg for lead ion and MB dye using pH 6 and 7, respectively. 10 mL of 10 mg L-1 of both pollutants were shaken with the nanocomposite for 30 min and %R value was then fulfilled as mentioned above [43]. Impact of concentration and study of the isotherm models were performed by mixing 10 mg of the nanocomposite with 10 mL of pollutant (5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 60, 80 and 100 mg L-1) at pH 6 and 7 for lead ion and MB, respectively. These were shaken with the nanocomposite for 30 min to identify the %R value as illustrated above [44]. Temperature impact on the adsorption process was done by mixing 10 mg of nanocomposite with 10 mL of 10 mg L-1 of lead ion and MB pollutants at pH 6 and 7, respectively and shaken for 30 min at four different temperatures (298, 308, 328 and 348 K). Determination of %R was then fulfilled as mentioned above and used to calculate the thermodynamic parameters. Effect of coexisting salts on the adsorption of pollutant were investigated by mixing 100 mg of NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl, CaCl2 and MgSO4 with 10 mg of the nanocomposite in 10 mL of 10 mg L-1 of both pollutants at their optimum pH and shaken for 30 min to determine the %R value as reported above [28,32].

Regeneration and reusability of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite after the initial use in removal of both pollutants was separately performed. The loaded pollutants on the nanocomposite were separated by filtration, washed and shaken with 50 mL of 0.1 mol/L hydrochloric acid for 1 h. The solid materials were then rinsed with distilled water to pH 7.0 and dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight and the regenerated nanocomposites were applied in further adsorption processes. The above steps were repeated five times to obtain five recycled nanocomposites and the percentage removal values of both pollutants were characterized after each cycle [20,21].

Application of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite in removal of both pollutants from real water samples as tap water, sea water and agriculture wastewater were investigated by using and mixing 10 mg nanocomposite with 100 mL of the spiked water samples with 1 mg L-1 of pollutant and shaken for 30 min. Determination of %R values were then fulfilled as mentioned above [28].

The formation of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite was initially confirmed from the FT-IR analysis as shown in Fig. 1 . The FT-IR spectrum of ZnO shows an ascribed peak at 3437 cm−1 to the stretching vibration of surface hydroxyl group. Moreover, two other peaks at 1119 and 1385 cm−1 are assigned to the primary and secondary OH groups for in-plane bending and vibrational modes, respectively. The peaks at 865–500 cm−1 are generally related to metal–oxygen bond via ZnO stretching vibrations. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs shows several assigned peaks at 3448, 2905, 1653, 1562 and 1415 cm−1 for OH stretching, CH stretching, conjugated CC or CO, NH stretching and CC of benzene ring, respectively. The FT-IR of ZnO/C-Foam exhibited a number of related peaks to OH stretching, CC–H stretching, COO– symmetric and asymmetric stretching as well as Zn-O at 3436, 3266, 1584 and 448 cm−1, respectively. Finally, the ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate was confirmed by the assignment of several peaks at 3370, 1602, 1426, 1028, 817 and 580 cm−1 for OH stretching, CC or CO, COO-symmetric and asymmetric stretching, CC of benzene ring, Zn-C stretching, and Zn-O, respectively. The collected data of various functional groups in the assembled ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate confirm the successful modification process of the aimed nanocomposite [45–47].

The formation of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate was also confirmed via the EDX analysis as illustrated in Fig. 2 by presence of C, N, O, Ca and Zn which are referred by mass (%) as 34.35, 0.69, 54.45, 6.02 and 1.74 %, respectively with the predominant oxygenated functional groups for enhancing the removal processes of the aimed Pb(II) and MB dye pollutants from aqueous matrices.

The SEM images and surface morphology of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite and its constituents are identified and demonstrated in Fig. 1S (Supplementary material). The SEM images of ZnO and calcium alginate showed agglomeration of the particles (Fig. 1Sa and 1Sb). In addition, the image of alginate is not uniformly distributed due to possible binding between calcium ions and alginate. It is apparent that the surface of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate is homogeneous and the prepared nanocomposite exhibited semi-spherical particles with large number of surface pores for efficient adsorption and removal process. The HR-TEM images of ZnO, calcium alginate, GQDs and ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate are represented in Fig. 3 with almost uniform shape and size of particles. However, the detected particles size of ZnO was 10 nm (Fig. 3a), while the HR-TEM image of calcium alginate confirms high agglomeration (Fig. 3b). The HR-TEM image of GQDs indicates spherical particles (Fig. 3c and 3d). Finally, the TEM image of assembled ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate is illustrated in Fig. 3e and 3f to show almost uniform shape and size of particles.

The XRD patterns of ZnO, calcium alginate, GQDs and ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite are provided in Fig. 2S (Supplementary material). The XRD pattern of ZnO (Fig. 2Sa) was detected as hexagonal phase as confirmed by JCPDS 76–0704 data. The assigned peaks and their relative intensities of ZnO were well matched with the literature values and no evidence for impurity characteristic peaks. The characterized ZnO peaks were located at 2⊖ = 31.67° (100), 34.98° (002), 36.70° (101), 46.2° (102), 57.13° (110), 63.2° (103) and 68.9° (112). The X-ray diffraction patterns of calcium alginate (Fig. 2Sb) exhibited a weak and broad diffraction peak at 2⊖ =15.6° to refer to an amorphous structure of calcium alginate. For GQDs, a characterized broad peak at 2⊖= 26.2° (002) near to graphite to confirm the restoration of destroyed network of carbon atoms after reduction (Fig. 5c). The broad peak in GQDs is mainly related to the larger d-spacing that confirms oxygen containing functional groups. The XRD patterns of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite show the existence of some characteristic peaks that are related to its constituents to further refer to the successful formation of the aimed ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite [48–52].

The thermal behavior of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate is illustrated in Fig. 3S (Supplementary material) to reveal high thermal stability of the synthesized nanocomposite by showing three plateaus. The first is due to a weight loss = 13.4 % at 165 °C and this step may be attributed to loss of moisture. The second is related to the loss of organic moiety especially carboxylate groups with weight loss = 20.4 % at 373 °C and the final step at 696 °C is correlated to the thermal degradation of the remaining organic moiety with 7.2 % weight loss [53].
Finally, the surface area of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/ alginate was determined by the BET method and found 38.9 m2/g with average pore diameter 7.8 nm and pore volume 0.25 cm3g−1.

The impact of pH on the contaminant removal process represents a major factor as it influences the responsible surface-active functional groups in the adsorption process. In this study, a leaching procedure was initially performed at different conditions (pH 1–9) to figure out the possible loss of ZnO and other constituents from the assembled ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs alginate and the collected results referred to excellent stability values of this nanocomposite up to >99.0 ± 4.0 %. Therefore, the influence of pH was performed in the entire range of pH 1.0–9.0. The removal effects of lead ions and methylene blue were found to be pH dependent as shown in Fig. 4a , for both pollutants. It was confirmed that as the pH of contact solution increased, the %R values increased to reach to the optimized pH 6 for lead ion with 96.2 % removal and pH 7 for MB with 92.4 % removal. At low pHs, a competition is predicted between protons and the cationic pollutants for the active sites on the nanocomposite. However, as the pH value increases, the functional groups tend to be deprotonated as the available carboxylate and/or amine groups for bonding with the two contaminants [54–56]. The removal values of Pb(II) and methylene blue were increased as the pH became > 5. This is in a good agreement with the detected pHPZC = 5.9 of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs alginate nanocomposite (Fig. 4S, Supplementary material). Above the pHPZC, the surface of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite was negatively charged to ease and facilitate the adsorption process of positively charged ions of lead and MB. However, at pH < pHPZC the nanocomposite surface was positively charged and thus repelled the investigated Pb(II) and methylene pollutants, in addition to the competing high concentration of proton ions in solution [57,58].

The mass dosage of nanocomposite is an important factor to investigate and monitor the adsorption capability of both pollutants onto the active sites and mass ratio of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite. The mass range 1.0–10.0 mg of nanocomposite was used along with 30 min shaking time and pH 6 and 7 for lead ion and MB dye, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 4b , the %R efficiency was increased as dosage of the nanocomposite increased. For example, at 1.0 mg dosage, the %R values were 76.7 % and 40.9 % for lead ion and MB dye, respectively owing to the low available active sites for adsorption compared to high amount of pollutants. Such removal values were sharply increased by using 8.0 mg dosage to reach to 95.5 % and 88.1 % for lead ion and MB dye, respectively. Finally upon increasing the mass of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite to 10.0 mg, high removal percentages of lead ion and MB dye were identified as 96.3 % and 92.5 %, respectively to provide a strong evidence for efficient and excellent binding of the two pollutants to the available active oxygen and nitrogen functional sites on the surface of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of time and kinetic studies
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of time on the removal processes of lead ion and MB dye by ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite was examined by using 10 mg nanocomposite at optimum pH for each pollutant at time rang 1–30 min and the results of this study are shown in Fig. 4c . In case of lead ion removal, the R% was found high from the beginning till 25 min by providing 96.3 % removal and finally reached to a steady state. For MB dye, it yielded a 16.4 % removal at 1 min followed by rapid increase to 91.8 % at 25 min as the optimum time and finally reached to 92.5 % removal at 30 min [60].

Regarding the kinetics study, several models as pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, intraparticle diffusion and Elovich kinetic models were applied as expressed and defined in Table 2S (Supplementary material). As listed in Table 3S (Supplementary material), the application of pseudo-first order model was characterized to give a poor correlation based on the qe(calculated) = 0.29 and 12.55 mg g−1 with R2 0.98 and 0.89 for lead ion and MB dye, respectively. For pseudo-second order model, qe(calculated) were found 9.64 and 11.31 with R2 = 1.00 and 0.99 for lead ion and MB dye, respectively which are close enough to qe(exp.) with high coefficients (R2) as also shown in Fig. 4c. So, it is evident that the chemical adsorption reactions were the acting mechanism and the removal processes were directly dependent on both adsorbate and adsorbents. The intraparticle diffusion model states that the adsorption process is dependent on two steps mechanism. The first one is related to the process of pollutant diffusion from the bulk solution into the surface of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite, while the second step is dealing with the incorporation of the pollutant into the pores of nanocomposite. Moreover, this model is also dependent on t1/2 rather than contact time. The computed Kid values were found 0.05 and 1.69 mg g−1 min−1/5, C values = 9.36 and 0.68 and R2 = 0.81 and 0.96 for lead ion and MB dye, respectively as compiled in Table 3S (Supplementary material). The fourth investigated model is the Elovich which is based on heterogeneous surface and chemisorption kinetics. The computed R2 values of lead ion and MB dye were found 0.87 and 0.98, respectively. From the above-mentioned results and correlation factors, it could be concluded that the removal processes of both pollutants were categorized to best fit with the pseudo-second order kinetics and the adsorption reactions were also in accordance with the Elovich model [61,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pollutant concentrations impact and adsorption isotherms
ce:section.ce:para: Impacts of the two pollutants concentration were studied using 10 mg of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite under 30 min shaking time at optimum pH for both pollutants. As shown in Fig. 4d , the %R of 5.0 mg L-1 lead ion was 96.5 % and this value was gradually decreased to 90.7 % by using 100 mg L-1 lead ion. Similar behavior was also characterized by studying the impact of concentration of MB as the identified %R was 92.8 % by using 5.0 mg L-1 MB dye and this value was decreased to 73.9 % upon using 100 mg L-1 MB dye concentration.

Linear and nonlinear isotherm expressions were studied and evaluated by four different models via Langmuir/Freundlich/Temkin/Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) to figure out the interaction mechanisms of both pollutants with the surface of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite [63–65]. The Langmuir illustrates uniform monolayer adsorption process on homogeneous surfaces without interaction between contaminants particles. As stated in Table 1a (Supplementary material) and shown in Fig. 4e , the linear Langmuir adsorption isotherm provided an excellent correlation according to R2 values 0.997 and 0.9965 for lead ion and MB dye, respectively. In addition, the qmax and b values were also calculated and compiled in Table 1a (Supplementary material). The separation factor RL is used to confirm the removal process, where the values 0 < RL < 1 refers to favorable process, RL = 0 refers to reversible removal process and RL > 1 denotes to unfavorable process and RL = 1 for linear process. As listed in Table 1a (Supplementary material), the computed RL values for both pollutants were found in the range 0–1 to indicate that the adsorption processes of the two pollutants on the surface of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite were related to uniform monolayers via chemisorption with the surface active oxygen and/or nitrogen functional groups [63–65]. The linear Freundlich model describes multilayer heterogeneous adsorption protocol. As listed in Table 1a (Supplementary material), the R2 values by this model were 0.98 and 0.95 for lead ion and MB dye, respectively. To additionally describe the interaction between the two pollutants with the nanocomposite, Temkin model was used which states that the heat of contaminants removal decreases linearly with increasing the surface coverage due to interaction between the nanocomposite surface and contaminant molecules. Accordingly, the linear Temkin model referred to R2 = 0.94 and 0.96 for lead ion and MB dye, respectively. The mechanism of adsorption was also investigated by the Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) isotherm model to describe the porosity in ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite and the removal energy. From Table 1a (Supplementary material), the identified R2 from the straight lines were 0.82 and 0.76 for Pb(II) and MB dye, respectively. Therefore, the above-mentioned results reveal that the removal of the two pollutants by ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite was best fitted to Langmuir adsorption isotherm model.

For better evaluation of the adsorption processes of both pollutants by ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite, nonlinear adsorption isotherms were also applied for the four adsorption isotherm models as represented in Fig. 4f and the computed nonlinear isotherm parameters are listed in Table 1b . The nonlinear Langmuir model revealed correlation factors R2 adj 0.9970 and 0.9965 and maximum adsorption capacity 133.8 and 91.0 mg g−1 for lead ion and MB dye, respectively. The nonlinear Freundlich model confirmed R2 adj 0.980 and 0.960 with nf 1.6 and 2.0 indicating favored adsorption processes by the two contaminants. The nonlinear Temkin model estimated the correlation factors R2 as 0.940 and 0.967 with high bT values corresponding to 127.0 and 147.7 J mol−1 to refer to strong interactions between the two contaminates and ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite. Finally, the nonlinear Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) isotherm model resulted in correlation factors R2 0.8247 and 0.7694 as compiled in Table 1b. The computed and collected results from the nonlinear adsorption isotherm expressions agreed well with the linear ones to conform that the adsorption processes of the two pollutants onto ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite were most fitted to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of temperature and thermodynamic study
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature on the removal of lead ion and MB dye by ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite was investigated at temperature ranging from 298 to 348 K and the thermodynamic parameters including ΔG°, ΔH°, and ΔS° were also determined. It is evident that as the temperature increases, the %R values of both pollutants by the ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite were also increased to indicate endothermic adsorption process by both contaminants. The characterized entropy (ΔSo) values were positive confirming elevation of disordering degree during the adsorption reactions. Based on the Van’t Hoff plot (Fig. 4g ), the ΔSo values were 0.11 and 0.04 kJ/mol K and the ΔHo values were found 25.91 and 5.39 kJ/mol for lead ion and MB dye, respectively as illustrated in Table 4S (Supplementary material). The computed ΔG° values were found negative for both pollutants at all temperatures indicating spontaneous adsorption reaction. ΔGo values were ranged from −8.06 to −13.67 kJ/mol and from −6.23 to −8.19 kJ/mol for lead ion and MB dye, respectively as previously described [58].

Fig. 4h illustrates the effect of interfering salts (NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl, CaCl2, and MgSO4) in the removal process of lead ion and MB dye by ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite and these salts exhibited slight or no effect on %R by both pollutants. The %R of the two pollutants showed slight decreases in presence of CaCl2, and MgSO4 and this may be attributed to their smaller ionic radii compared to monovalent cations to confirm some sorts of competition for the active sited on the surface of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite [66].

The regeneration step is an important procedure for economic and application reasons. It was fulfilled by desorption of the adsorbed pollutant from the surface of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite by using hydrochloric acid and followed by washing and drying to further use of the recycled nanocomposite. Initially, a leaching procedure was performed in different conditions (pH 1–9) to figure out the possible loss of adsorbed ZnO as well as other constituents from the assembled ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs alginate. The collected results referred to excellent stability values of this nanocomposite up to >99.0 ± 4.0 %. Therefore, 0.1 mol/L HCl (pH 1.0) was applied in this study as a regeneration reagent. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the first recycled ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite produced effective %R for both Pb(II) and MB dye pollutants with 97.9 % and 93.5 %, respectively. In addition, after five successive regeneration processes of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite, the %R values were identified as 90.3 % and 70.5 % for lead ion and MB dye, respectively. Therefore, the outlined results from this study point out to the successful reusability of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite in multipurpose applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of pollutants from real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the spiked real water samples with both pollutants were mixed with 10 mg of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate and shaken for 30 min. The %R values of lead ion by ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite were reached to 100 %, 100 % and 83.6 % from tap water, agriculture wastewater and sea water, while the %R of MB dye were 92.2 %, 91.1 % and 81.7 % from tap water, agriculture wastewater and sea water, respectively. It is obvious that the %R showed slight decrease in case of sea water due to the presence of some competing ions as Ca(II) and Mg(II) in sea water [58] . This behavior denotes to the superior applicability of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite for removal of the two pollutants from real water samples.

A novel ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite was designed and prepared via cost-effective procedure for the removal of lead ion and MB dye pollutants from aquatic matrices. Surface and structural confirmations of the assembled nanocomposite were established to refer to 20 nm average particle size of the nanocomposite. It was also confirmed that the optimum pH values for removal of lead ion and MB dye pollutants were 6 and 7, respectively. The results of linear and nonlinear adsorption isotherm models denoted that the removal processes of the two pollutants onto ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite were best matching with the Langmuir model. The chemical adsorption reaction was confirmed as the acting mechanism for both pollutants and the removal processes were directly dependent on both adsorbate and adsorbents as concluded from the pseudo-second-order models. The computed thermodynamic parameters for the adsorption process were based on endothermic and spontaneous reactions with ΔGo ranging from −8.06 to −13.67 kJ/mol and from −6.23 to −8.19 kJ /mol for lead ion and MB dye pollutants, respectively. The investigated ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate nanocomposite exhibited successful reusability for five successive regeneration cycles. The impacts of interfering ions and salts were almost neglected. Finally, the applicability of the as-prepared ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate for removal of lead ion and MB dye pollutants from real water samples was investigated and successfully produced 100 % and 92.2 % removal values, respectively. Finally, the practical performance of ZnO/C-Foam/GQDs/Alginate was compared versus other previously reported adsorbents as compiled in Table 2 .
